name: Albania
tagline: From Illyrian tribes and Skanderbeg's mountain resistance to a small Balkan state forged on the edge of empires.
summary: Albania traces its identity to the ancient Illyrians, whose lands along the eastern Adriatic passed under Rome, Byzantium and finally the Ottomans. In the fifteenth century the warrior prince Gjergj Kastrioti, known as Skanderbeg, held the Ottomans off from his stronghold at Kruje for a quarter-century, but after his death the country was absorbed into the Ottoman Empire for nearly five hundred years, during which most Albanians embraced Islam. A nineteenth-century cultural and political revival, the Rilindja, and the League of Prizren of 1878 prepared the ground for statehood, and on 28 November 1912 Ismail Qemali proclaimed independence at Vlore amid the Balkan Wars. The Great Powers recognised Albania at the Conference of London but drew its borders so as to leave roughly half of the Albanian-populated lands, above all Kosovo, outside the new state, creating the enduring Albanian national question. After the reign of King Zog, an Italian invasion in 1939 and the Second World War, the country fell under the long Stalinist dictatorship of Enver Hoxha, who ruled in extreme isolation until 1985. Communism collapsed in 1990-91; after a turbulent transition that included the near-anarchy of 1997, Albania joined NATO in 2009 and became a candidate for European Union membership in 2014.

founding.label: The Declaration of Independence at Vlore
founding.detail: Modern Albania dates its statehood to 28 November 1912, when the assembly gathered at Vlore under Ismail Qemali proclaimed Albania an independent and sovereign state and raised the national flag, breaking away from the Ottoman Empire during the First Balkan War. The new state's existence and frontiers were confirmed by the Great Powers at the Conference of London in 1912-13, which recognised an autonomous principality of Albania but, under pressure from Serbia, Montenegro and Greece, fixed its borders so that a large share of the Albanian-populated territory, including Kosovo, was left outside the country. The independence won at Vlore was thus both the founding act of the Albanian state and the origin of a national question that has shaped Albanian politics ever since.

quickFacts.label: Ancient roots
quickFacts.value: Illyrian tribes and the Greco-Roman city of Butrint (Buthrotum), a UNESCO World Heritage Site

quickFacts.label: National hero
quickFacts.value: Skanderbeg (Gjergj Kastrioti), who resisted the Ottomans from Kruje, c. 1443-1468

quickFacts.label: Ottoman rule
quickFacts.value: Roughly five centuries, from the late 15th century until 1912; most Albanians became Muslim

quickFacts.label: Borders and the national question
quickFacts.value: Conference of London (1912-13) left about half the Albanian-populated lands, above all Kosovo, outside the state

quickFacts.label: NATO and EU
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO on 1 April 2009; granted EU candidate status in June 2014

eras.title: Illyrians, Greeks, Rome and Byzantium
eras.standfirst: On the eastern shore of the Adriatic the Illyrians built the deep substratum of Albanian identity, their cities passing in turn to Greek colonists, Rome and the Byzantine Empire.
body: Albanians traditionally trace their origins to the Illyrians, an ancient people who occupied the western Balkans and whose tribes and small kingdoms held the lands along the eastern Adriatic from the second half of the first millennium BCE. Greek colonists settled coastal sites from the eighth century BCE, among them Buthrotum, today Butrint, which grew from an Illyrian and Epirote settlement into a flourishing Greco-Roman city. In the second century BCE the Illyrians were conquered by Rome, and their territory was organised within the Roman provincial system; over the following centuries Latin influence reshaped the region while elements of the older Illyrian tongue survived in what would become Albanian.

Butrint embodies this long layering of civilisations. Inhabited over many centuries, it preserves the remains of an Epirote-Greek theatre, a Roman forum and aqueduct, an early Christian baptistery with mosaic floors and later Byzantine and Venetian fortifications. The site, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1992, is the most important archaeological complex in Albania and a record in stone of the Illyrian, Greek, Roman, Byzantine and Venetian presence on this coast.

With the division of the Roman world the Albanian lands fell to the Eastern, Byzantine, empire, ruled from Constantinople. For most of the next eight centuries they remained a Byzantine borderland, contested at times by Bulgarians, Normans and others, and crossed by the great Roman road, the Via Egnatia, linking the Adriatic to the East. Christianity took root here early, and the population was gradually drawn into the Orthodox and, along the coast, Latin Christian worlds, even as the Slavic migrations of the early medieval centuries transformed the surrounding Balkans.

events.title: Illyrian and Greek Butrint
events.summary: Illyrian tribes inhabited the eastern Adriatic coast, where Greek colonists from about the eighth century BCE helped develop the settlement of Buthrotum (Butrint) into a flourishing city.

events.title: Roman conquest of Illyria
events.summary: Rome conquered the Illyrians in the second century BCE and absorbed their lands; Butrint flourished under Roman rule with a forum, aqueduct and public buildings.

events.title: Byzantine rule begins
events.summary: After the division of the Roman Empire the Albanian lands passed to the Byzantine Empire, remaining a contested Christian borderland for much of the following eight centuries.

events.title: Early Christian Butrint
events.summary: Butrint became an episcopal seat with an early Christian basilica and a baptistery whose mosaic floor survives, marking the spread of Christianity in the region under Byzantium.

figures.name: The Illyrians
figures.role: Ancient people of the western Balkans
figures.blurb: A cluster of tribes and kingdoms on the eastern Adriatic, the Illyrians are regarded in Albanian tradition as the ancestors of the Albanians; their language is thought to underlie modern Albanian, and Rome conquered them in the second century BCE.

figures.name: Butrint (Buthrotum)
figures.role: Ancient city and World Heritage Site
figures.blurb: An Illyrian and Greek foundation that became a Roman colony and later a Byzantine and Venetian stronghold, Butrint preserves theatres, basilicas and fortifications spanning antiquity to the Middle Ages and was inscribed by UNESCO in 1992.

pullquote.text: Inhabited since prehistoric times, Butrint has been the site of a Greek colony, a Roman city and a bishopric, a microcosm of Mediterranean history.
pullquote.attribution: On the layered past of Butrint

eras.title: Medieval Albania, the Ottoman Conquest and Skanderbeg
eras.standfirst: Out of the decline of Byzantium rose Albanian principalities, soon faced with the advancing Ottomans; for a quarter-century Skanderbeg held them off from his fortress at Kruje.
body: As Byzantine power waned after the twelfth century, the Albanian lands fragmented among local lords and were fought over by Serbian, Angevin, Venetian and other powers. A short-lived medieval Albanian principality emerged in the region, and noble families such as the Thopia, Balsha, Kastrioti and Arianiti carved out lordships. Into this divided land came the Ottoman Turks: they invaded Albania in 1388 and, despite resistance, brought most of the country under their control over the following decades, completing the conquest around 1430.

The most celebrated chapter of Albanian history belongs to Gjergj Kastrioti, known as Skanderbeg. Son of an Albanian lord, he had been taken as a hostage to the Ottoman court, converted to Islam and trained as a military commander, receiving the name Iskander (Skanderbeg). In 1443 he broke with the Ottomans, returned to Christianity and seized his ancestral stronghold of Kruje. The following year he united Albanian princes in the League of Lezhe and led a long war of resistance. For some twenty-five years, operating from the mountain town of Kruje, he frustrated every attempt by the much larger Ottoman armies to subdue Albania, repelling sieges of Kruje in 1450 and again in 1466-67 and winning support from the papacy, Naples, Venice and other Italian powers.

Skanderbeg's struggle made him a hero across Christian Europe and a lasting symbol of Albanian national identity, but it could not be sustained after his death at Lezhe in 1468. Deprived of his leadership and divided among themselves, the Albanian lords were overwhelmed; the Ottomans took Kruje in 1478 and the Venetian-held port of Shkoder in 1479, completing the absorption of Albania into the Ottoman Empire and beginning nearly five centuries of Ottoman rule.

events.title: Ottoman invasion of Albania
events.summary: The Ottoman Turks invaded Albania in 1388 and, over the following decades, brought most of the divided and contested country under their control, largely completing the conquest by about 1430.

events.title: Skanderbeg's revolt and the return to Kruje
events.summary: Gjergj Kastrioti (Skanderbeg) broke with the Ottomans after 1443, reconverted to Christianity and seized his ancestral fortress of Kruje, launching a national revolt against Ottoman rule.

events.title: League of Lezhe
events.summary: Skanderbeg united Albanian princes in the League of Lezhe to coordinate resistance, giving the scattered lordships a measure of common purpose against the Ottoman Empire.

events.title: The sieges of Kruje
events.summary: From his stronghold at Kruje Skanderbeg withstood repeated Ottoman sieges, including those of 1450 and 1466-67, keeping much of Albania free for some twenty-five years.

events.title: Death of Skanderbeg
events.summary: Skanderbeg died at Lezhe in 1468; without his leadership Albanian resistance collapsed, and the Ottomans took Kruje in 1478 and Shkoder in 1479, completing the conquest.

figures.name: Skanderbeg (Gjergj Kastrioti)
figures.blurb: Albania's national hero, Skanderbeg defected from Ottoman service in 1443, seized Kruje and for a quarter-century led the Albanian resistance, frustrating every Ottoman attempt to reconquer the country and winning the admiration and aid of Christian Europe.

figures.name: Sultan Mehmed II
figures.blurb: The conqueror of Constantinople, Mehmed II personally led campaigns against Skanderbeg, including the great siege of Kruje in 1466-67; only after Skanderbeg's death did Ottoman forces finally subdue Albania.

pullquote.text: For 25 years, operating out of his stronghold in the mountain town of Kruje, Skanderbeg frustrated every attempt by the Turks to regain Albania.

eras.title: Five Centuries of Ottoman Rule and the National Awakening
eras.standfirst: Nearly five hundred years within the Ottoman Empire reshaped Albania's religion and society; in the nineteenth century a cultural and political revival turned a people into a nation.
body: Under Ottoman rule, which lasted from the late fifteenth century until 1912, Albania was administered as a frontier province and largely cut off from the currents of Western Europe. Over the following centuries a majority of Albanians converted to Islam, while substantial Orthodox and Catholic Christian communities persisted, giving the country its distinctive mix of faiths. Albanians rose high in Ottoman service as soldiers, administrators and grand viziers, and powerful local pashas, most famously Ali Pasha of Tepelene, carved out semi-independent domains. The towns of Berat and Gjirokastra, whose well-preserved Ottoman-era architecture led UNESCO to inscribe their historic centres as a World Heritage Site, are monuments to this long period.

The nineteenth century brought a national revival known as the Rilindja, the Albanian rebirth or awakening. Writers and patriots such as the poet Naim Frasheri and his brothers worked to standardise the Albanian language, create a national literature and assert a distinct Albanian identity distinct from Ottoman, Greek and Slavic neighbours. The decisive political moment came in 1878, when the Treaty of San Stefano and the Congress of Berlin threatened to award Albanian-inhabited districts to neighbouring states. In response Albanian leaders founded the League of Prizren on 1 July 1878, which sought to unite the Albanian lands into a single autonomous unit within the empire and to defend them against partition.

The League of Prizren was suppressed by the Ottomans by 1881, but it had given organised form to Albanian nationalism and set its goals for the next generation: protection of the Albanian lands, autonomy and the cultivation of the national language and culture. As the Ottoman Empire weakened and its Balkan neighbours expanded, Albanians increasingly understood that only their own statehood could secure them against being divided among Serbia, Montenegro, Greece and Bulgaria, the fear that would be realised in the wars of 1912-13.

events.title: Albania within the Ottoman Empire
events.summary: Following the fall of Kruje and Shkoder, Albania was incorporated into the Ottoman Empire, beginning nearly five centuries of rule during which most Albanians converted to Islam.

events.title: Ottoman towns of Berat and Gjirokastra
events.summary: The towns of Berat and Gjirokastra developed the distinctive Ottoman-era urban architecture, including fortified citadels and tall stone houses, that later earned them UNESCO World Heritage status.

events.title: The Rilindja national awakening
events.summary: A cultural and political revival, the Rilindja, promoted the Albanian language, literature and a distinct national identity, led by writers and patriots such as the poet Naim Frasheri.

events.title: League of Prizren
events.summary: Founded at Prizren in response to the Congress of Berlin, the Albanian League sought to unify the Albanian lands into one autonomous unit and to resist their partition among neighbouring states.

figures.name: Naim Frasheri
figures.blurb: Regarded as Albania's national poet, Naim Frasheri was a leading figure of the Rilindja, writing works that celebrated Albanian history and language and helped forge a modern national consciousness.

figures.name: Abdyl Frasheri
figures.role: Leader of the League of Prizren
figures.blurb: Brother of the poet Naim, Abdyl Frasheri was a leading political voice of the national awakening and a driving force of the League of Prizren, championing Albanian autonomy within the Ottoman Empire.

pullquote.text: The Albanian League strove to unify all the Albanian territories into one autonomous Albania and spearheaded a movement to develop Albanian language, literature, education, and culture.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the League of Prizren

eras.title: Independence, the Conference of London and the Albanian Question
eras.standfirst: Independence proclaimed at Vlore was secured only as the Great Powers drew borders that left half the Albanians outside the state; a fragile kingdom followed, ended by Italian invasion.
body: As the Balkan Wars broke up Ottoman rule in Europe, Albanian leaders moved to claim statehood before their lands could be partitioned. On 28 November 1912 an assembly at Vlore led by Ismail Qemali proclaimed Albania independent and raised the national flag, establishing a provisional government with Qemali as its head. The new state's survival, however, depended on the European Great Powers, who took up the question at the Conference of London (also called the Conference of Ambassadors) that met from December 1912. The powers agreed in 1913 to recognise an autonomous Principality of Albania, and the Treaty of London of 30 May 1913 confirmed the Ottoman cession of its remaining European territories.

The borders the powers fixed were a lasting grievance. Under pressure from Serbia, Montenegro and Greece, the Conference of London left roughly half of the Albanian-populated lands, and a large share of the Albanian population, outside the new state, assigning the Albanian-inhabited region of Kosovo to Serbia and parts of the south, including Chameria, toward Greece. This partition created the enduring Albanian national question, the unresolved status of the many Albanians left beyond the country's frontiers, above all in Kosovo and western Macedonia. The first years of independence were chaotic: a brief reign by the German prince William of Wied collapsed within months, and the First World War saw Albania occupied by rival armies.

Order returned in the 1920s under Ahmed Zogu, who dominated Albanian politics, became president in 1925 and in 1928 proclaimed himself King Zog I. His authoritarian monarchy modernised parts of the state but drew Albania ever closer to Fascist Italy, which extended loans and influence. The relationship ended in conquest: on 7 April 1939 Italy invaded Albania, King Zog fled into exile, and the Italian king Victor Emmanuel III was declared king of Albania, ending the country's independence on the eve of the Second World War.

events.title: Declaration of independence at Vlore
events.summary: An assembly at Vlore led by Ismail Qemali proclaimed Albania an independent state and raised the national flag, breaking from the Ottoman Empire during the First Balkan War.

events.title: Conference of London and the borders
events.summary: The Great Powers recognised an autonomous Albania but, pressed by its neighbours, drew borders leaving about half the Albanian-populated lands, including Kosovo, outside the state, creating the Albanian national question.

events.title: The rise of Zog
events.summary: Ahmed Zogu consolidated power, becoming president in 1925 and in 1928 proclaiming himself King Zog I, ruling an authoritarian monarchy increasingly dependent on Fascist Italy.

events.title: Italian invasion of Albania
events.summary: Fascist Italy invaded Albania on 7 April 1939; King Zog fled into exile and the Italian king Victor Emmanuel III was proclaimed king of Albania, ending its independence.

figures.name: Ismail Qemali
figures.blurb: A veteran Ottoman statesman, Ismail Qemali led the assembly at Vlore that proclaimed Albanian independence on 28 November 1912 and headed the first provisional government of the new state.

figures.name: King Zog I
figures.role: President and King of the Albanians
figures.blurb: Born Ahmed Zogu, he dominated interwar Albania, becoming president in 1925 and king in 1928. His monarchy modernised the state but fell to the Italian invasion of 1939, after which he lived in exile.

pullquote.text: The Conference of London recognised an autonomous Albania, but its borders left roughly half of the Albanian-populated lands, above all Kosovo, outside the new state.
pullquote.attribution: On the origin of the Albanian national question

eras.title: The Second World War and Hoxha's Stalinist Albania
eras.standfirst: Out of resistance to Italian and German occupation came a communist victory; for over four decades Enver Hoxha ruled the most isolated and tightly controlled state in Europe.
body: Italian rule gave way to German occupation after Italy's surrender in 1943, and resistance was led above all by the communist-dominated National Liberation Movement. When the Germans withdrew in 1944 the partisans took Tirana and established a provisional government headed by Enver Hoxha, the founder and leader of the Albanian Communist Party. The United States, which had recognised Albania in 1922 and severed relations after the Italian takeover in 1939, did not restore them, and Albania disappeared behind an ideological wall that would last more than four decades.

Hoxha transformed Albania into an Eastern European model of Stalinism. His regime nationalised the economy, collectivised agriculture and imposed one of the most repressive systems in the communist world, imprisoning, executing or exiling landowners, clan leaders, clergy of all faiths and party officials judged disloyal; in 1967 the state went so far as to proclaim Albania the world's first officially atheist country and to close its mosques and churches. Hoxha's foreign policy was a sequence of ruptures: he broke with Tito's Yugoslavia in 1948, allied with the Soviet Union, then broke with Moscow in 1961 after denouncing Khrushchev's de-Stalinisation, turning to Maoist China before breaking with Beijing as well in the late 1970s.

The result was an Albania sealed off from both East and West, its borders fortified, foreign travel forbidden and the country dotted with hundreds of thousands of defensive bunkers. Hoxha forced a backward, semi-feudal society into a centrally planned industrial economy at enormous human cost, building a personality cult and a pervasive secret police. He ruled until his death in 1985, leaving behind the poorest and most isolated state in Europe, governed by his chosen successor Ramiz Alia.

events.title: Communist victory and Hoxha in power
events.summary: As German forces withdrew, the communist-led partisans took Tirana and formed a provisional government under Enver Hoxha, who would rule Albania for over four decades.

events.title: Breaks with Yugoslavia and the USSR
events.summary: Hoxha broke with Tito's Yugoslavia in 1948 and allied with the Soviet Union, then ruptured with Moscow in 1961 over de-Stalinisation, turning instead to Maoist China.

events.title: Albania declared an atheist state
events.summary: The Hoxha regime launched a campaign against religion, closing mosques and churches and proclaiming Albania the world's first officially atheist state.

events.title: Rupture with China and total isolation
events.summary: Hoxha broke with post-Maoist China in the late 1970s, leaving Albania almost completely isolated, its borders sealed and its economy self-reliant by decree.

events.title: Death of Enver Hoxha
events.summary: Hoxha died in 1985 after ruling for over forty years, leaving the most tightly controlled society in Europe to his successor Ramiz Alia.

figures.name: Enver Hoxha
figures.blurb: Founder of the Albanian Communist Party and ruler from 1944 to 1985, Hoxha imposed a Stalinist regime of extreme repression and isolation, breaking successively with Yugoslavia, the Soviet Union and China and proclaiming Albania an atheist state.

figures.name: Ramiz Alia
figures.role: Hoxha's successor and last communist leader
figures.blurb: Hoxha's chosen heir, Alia led Albania after 1985 and presided over the unravelling of communist rule, conceding political pluralism in late 1990 under mounting popular pressure.

pullquote.text: Hoxha forced its transformation from a semifeudal relic of the Ottoman Empire into an industrialized economy with the most tightly controlled society in Europe.

eras.title: Transition, the 1997 Crisis, NATO and Europe
eras.standfirst: From the fall of communism through the near-collapse of 1997, Albania rebuilt a democratic state, joined NATO and sought European Union membership while its ties to Kosovo deepened.
body: Albania was among the last European states to abandon communism. Student protests in Tirana in December 1990 forced Ramiz Alia's party to authorise political pluralism, and the first multiparty elections were held in March 1991. The Party of Labour's continuous rule since 1944 ended with the elections of March 1992. The United States, whose relations with Albania had lapsed in 1939, resumed diplomatic relations on 15 March 1991, signalling the country's return to the wider world. The transition, however, was painful: in early 1997 the collapse of fraudulent pyramid investment schemes, in which a large part of the population had lost its savings, triggered armed unrest that spread from the south and brought the state close to anarchy before order was restored with international help.

Foreign relations slowly normalised. The long-formal state of war with Greece, a legacy of the Second World War and of competing claims over the south, where a Greek minority lives and which Greek nationalists called Northern Epirus, was only belatedly ended, Greece lifting it in the late 1980s; the related Cham question, concerning Albanians expelled from Greek Epirus after the war, remains a point of historical contention. Above all, Albania's fate stayed bound to Kosovo, whose population is overwhelmingly ethnic Albanian and which had been left outside Albania in 1913. The repression of Kosovo's Albanians under Yugoslavia and the war of the late 1990s drew Albania into the Kosovo question; Kosovo declared independence on 17 February 2008, recognised the next day by the United States.

Reorienting decisively toward the West, Albania joined NATO together with Croatia on 1 April 2009, becoming one of the alliance's newest members. It applied for European Union membership in April 2009 and was granted candidate status in June 2014; after years of delay, accession negotiations were formally opened in July 2022. A poor and long-isolated country at the start of the 1990s, Albania has rebuilt a democratic, market-oriented state firmly anchored in the Euro-Atlantic institutions, even as the Albanian national question first posed in 1913 continues to shape the politics of the region.

events.title: End of one-party rule
events.summary: After the Tirana student protests of December 1990, Albania held its first multiparty elections in March 1991, and the Party of Labour's monopoly on power ended with the 1992 elections.

events.title: US-Albania relations resumed
events.summary: The United States and Albania resumed diplomatic relations on 15 March 1991 after a break of more than half a century dating to the Italian takeover of 1939.

events.title: Pyramid scheme collapse and unrest
events.summary: The collapse of fraudulent pyramid investment schemes in early 1997 wiped out the savings of many Albanians and triggered armed unrest that brought the state close to anarchy before order was restored.

events.title: Kosovo declares independence
events.summary: Kosovo, whose population is overwhelmingly ethnic Albanian and which was left outside Albania in 1913, declared independence on 17 February 2008, recognised the next day by the United States.

events.title: Albania joins NATO
events.summary: Albania, with Croatia, formally joined NATO on 1 April 2009 as the alliance's 27th and 28th members, completing its strategic reorientation toward the West.

events.title: EU candidate status
events.summary: Albania, which applied for membership in April 2009, was granted European Union candidate status in June 2014; accession negotiations were formally opened in July 2022.

figures.name: Ramiz Alia
figures.blurb: Successor to Hoxha, Alia conceded political pluralism in December 1990 under popular pressure, opening the way to multiparty elections and the end of one-party rule in Albania.

figures.name: Ismail Qemali
figures.blurb: The founder of independent Albania remains the emblem of the unfinished national question: the state he proclaimed at Vlore in 1912 left half the Albanian nation, above all in Kosovo, beyond its 1913 borders.

pullquote.text: Because of pressure from its neighbours, roughly half of the territory and population claimed for Albania in 1913 was left outside the state, above all in Kosovo, whose people are overwhelmingly ethnic Albanian.
pullquote.attribution: On Albania, Kosovo and the national question

figures.name: Skanderbeg (Gjergj Kastrioti)
figures.blurb: The defining figure of Albanian history, Skanderbeg led the fifteenth-century resistance to the Ottomans from his fortress at Kruje, becoming a symbol of national identity and freedom whose memory inspired later generations.

figures.name: Naim Frasheri
figures.blurb: The foremost writer of the Albanian national awakening, Frasheri shaped the modern Albanian language and literature and gave voice to a distinct national consciousness in the nineteenth century.

figures.name: Ismail Qemali
figures.blurb: Ismail Qemali led the assembly that proclaimed Albanian independence at Vlore on 28 November 1912 and headed its first provisional government, founding the modern Albanian state.

figures.name: Enver Hoxha
figures.blurb: Ruler of Albania from 1944 to 1985, Hoxha built one of the most repressive and isolated regimes in Europe, breaking in turn with Yugoslavia, the Soviet Union and China and proclaiming an atheist state.
